- Action model: Same as Level 1 - click cross → direction → click target.
- Plan: First, try jumping the leftmost cross RIGHT over the adjacent cross.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see! After clicking the leftmost cross and pressing RIGHT, the g markers appeared at (16.5, 26.5) - this is the target position. The cross at col 14.5 can jump RIGHT over the cross at col 20.5 to land at col 26.5 (which is the empty cell between crosses at 20.5 and 32.5).
But then after pressing RIGHT again, the g markers disappeared and the board changed. It seems like pressing RIGHT twice caused something to happen. Let me check the current state - maybe the jump was executed!
Wait, looking at the output more carefully:
1. After click: G at (16.5, 14.5) and g markers at (16.5, 26.5) - showing the target
2. After RIGHT: No G or g markers, board changed
So pressing RIGHT a second time executed the jump! This is different from Level 1 where I had to click the target position. In Level 2, maybe the workflow is: click cross → direction → direction again to execute?
Or maybe pressing RIGHT just moved the selection and the jump happened automatically. Let me check the current cross positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current cross positions after the jump
seg = current_frame.segmentation
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green crosses: {len(green)}")
for n in green:
    center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
    print(f"  center={center}")

# Check for any other markers
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'g', 'O', 'Y']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  center=(16.5, 14.5)
  center=(16.5, 20.5)
  center=(16.5, 32.5)
  center=(16.5, 44.5)

  Y: center=(34.5, 44.5), pixels=20
  O: center=(34.5, 44.5), pixels=16

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The crosses didn't change - all 5 are still in the same positions. But the yellow/orange marker moved from (34.5, 32.5) to (34.5, 44.5). So pressing RIGHT didn't execute a jump - it moved the yellow/orange target marker!
This changes my understanding of the game. The yellow/orange cell is a separate entity that moves independently. It seems like:
1. Click a cross to select it
2. Press directions to move the yellow/orange target marker
3. When the target marker is on a valid landing spot, clicking it executes the jump
So the workflow might be:
1. Click a cross (selects it, shows G ring)
2. Press directions to move the target marker (Y/O cell)
3. Click the target marker to execute the jump
Let me verify this by trying to move the target marker to a valid jump position and then clicking it. The leftmost cross at (16.5, 14.5) can jump RIGHT over (16.5, 20.5) to land at (16.5, 26.5). The target marker is currently at (34.5, 44.5). I need to move it to (16.5, 26.5).
Wait, but the target marker is at row 34.5 which is between the panels. Maybe it can only move in certain areas? Let me try moving it LEFT to get closer to the target position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The yellow/orange marker moved from (34.5, 32.5) to (34.5, 44.5) when I pressed RIGHT
# It seems like the marker moves independently. Let me try to move it to a valid jump target.
# First, let me select a cross and see where the marker goes

# Select the cross at (16.5, 20.5) and try LEFT
predict(expect_change=True, note='Select cross at col 20.5')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 16, 'col': 20}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After selecting cross at 20.5:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'g', 'O', 'Y']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try LEFT to see target
predict(expect_change=True, note='LEFT from cross at 20.5')
action(['LEFT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['G', 'g', 'O', 'Y']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        print(f"  {n['color']}: center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
